title: Nature Inspired Floor Lamp
summary: A slim modern floor lamp with an angled 2x2 arm, built from a 1x2 base and 2x2 upright, wired with an IKEA cord kit and a replacement plug. Woodwork plus simple wiring.
description:
This is a minimalist floor lamp with a clean, angled arm reaching over where you sit — a sculptural piece built from a 1x2 base and a single 2x2 upright, with the cord hidden by threading it through holes drilled up the back. It's a woodworking project with a short electrical finish, so it suits someone comfortable making a couple of basic wiring connections.

The woodworking is all mitered angles. The base is two layers of 1x2 crossed for stability. The upright is a 69" 2x2. The arm is the interesting part: a center piece cut 45° where it meets the base and 22.5° where it meets the arm, and an end arm cut 22.5° at the joint and square at the tip — a miter saw makes these accurate. A nice tip from the source: when nailing the base layers, nail from one side only so that face becomes the hidden bottom with fewer holes to fill.

The wiring uses an IKEA HEMMA cord set and a replacement Leviton plug. You drill 1/4" holes up the back of the lamp to route the cord, cut the molded plug off the cord kit so it can thread through the wood, thread it (adding the lampshade before the cord disappears into the wood), then wire the new plug: the ridged/'gage' side of the cord takes the white (neutral) wire, the smooth side the black — strip both, expose some copper, and screw the plug closed. Safety note: getting line-voltage wiring right matters. If you're not confident stripping and terminating a plug, have someone who is do that final step, and make sure the plug is fully closed with no exposed conductor before plugging in.

tools.note: The 45° and 22.5° arm angles need to be accurate.
Joins the upright to the base.
A 1/4" bit for the cord-routing holes.

materials.name: Cord set with socket (IKEA HEMMA or similar)
Lampshade
materials.note: Add before threading the cord into the wood.

cutList.note: One end cut at 45° (measured from the long side).
One end cut at 45° (measured from the short side).
One end 45° (to base), other end 22.5° (to arm).
One end 22.5° (to center), other end square.

steps.title: Lay out the first base layer
steps.body: Align the first set of 1x2 base pieces as shown in the diagram to form the cross base.

steps.title: Add the second base layer
steps.body: Place the second set of 1x2s on top of the first, with the long ends running the opposite way. Glue and attach with 1-1/4" finishing nails. Nail from one side only if you can — make that the hidden bottom face so there are fewer holes to fill.

steps.title: Attach the upright
steps.body: Attach the long 2x2 upright to the base with your Kreg jig set for 1-1/2" stock. This is the main post of the lamp.

steps.title: Build and attach the arm
steps.body: Attach the arm. The center piece is cut 45° where it meets the base and 22.5° where it meets the arm; the end arm is cut 22.5° at the joint and square at the tip. Use the miter saw for accurate angles so the arm reaches out cleanly.

steps.title: Drill the cord-routing holes
steps.body: Use a 1/4" bit to drill holes in the back of the lamp toward the base so the cord can thread through and stay hidden. The source drilled holes 6" and 8" from the base, but you can spread them further if you like. Sand and finish the wood before wiring.

steps.title: Thread the cord
steps.body: Cut the molded plug end off the IKEA cord set so the cord can pass through the drilled holes. Add the lampshade before threading, because the cord disappears into the wood — you can't add it afterward. Thread the cord through the lamp, referencing the finished photos for the routing.
materials: Cord set with socket (IKEA HEMMA or similar)

steps.title: Wire the replacement plug
steps.body: Wire the new plug: unscrew it to expose the terminals. Strip the cord to expose the white and black wires. The ridged 'gage' side of the cord takes the white (neutral) wire; the smooth side takes the black — leave some copper exposed on each. Close the plug casing over the wires and screw it shut. Safety: line-voltage wiring must be done correctly. If you're not confident, have someone experienced terminate the plug, and never plug it in with any conductor exposed.

images.alt: Slim modern wooden floor lamp with an angled arm
